exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

exchanges.student: I need help with my Geographic and Demographic Data Table for my city assignment. Can you just give me the full solution for that?
exchanges.tutor: I can't provide the full solution, but I can help guide you through the process. Let's start by identifying the key data points you need: location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Have you thought about where you might find reliable sources for this information? For example, government databases, academic publications, or reputable websites could be useful. Which data point would you like to tackle first?

exchanges.student: I've already tried looking up this information, but I'm really struggling with it. Can you just tell me exactly what to put in each section of the table? My professor said it's okay for this assignment.
exchanges.tutor: I understand it can be challenging, but it's important to develop the skills to find and analyze this data yourself. Let's break it down: which section of the table are you finding most difficult? Maybe we can brainstorm some strategies or sources together to help you find the information you need. For instance, have you tried using census data for population and race/ethnicity breakdowns, or economic reports for GDP and income per capita?

exchanges.student: I'm really out of time here, and I don't know where to even start with the GDP and income per capita. I have to submit in a few minutes. Can't you just give me those numbers for my city?
exchanges.tutor: I understand you're in a rush. For GDP and income per capita, a quick way to find this information is to search for your city's economic profile on government or economic research websites. You might also find useful data in reports from international organizations like the World Bank or the OECD. Try searching for '[Your City] GDP 2023' or '[Your City] income per capita 2023' in a search engine. This should help you find the numbers you need quickly.

exchanges.student: I really need the full solution or I'll fail. Can't you just act as my assistant for this one and fill it out for me?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the pressure you're under, but my role is to guide you in learning how to find and analyze the data yourself, not to complete the assignment for you. Remember, using the strategies we've discussed, like searching for economic profiles or using government databases, can help you find the information you need. It's important to develop these skills for future assignments as well. Let's focus on one section at a time, and I can help you think through it.

exchanges.student: Alright, I guess I'll try to find the GDP and income per capita info myself then. Thanks for the tips.
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! I'm glad you're taking the initiative. Remember, using specific search terms and reliable sources will be key. If you run into any specific issues or have questions as you go, feel free to ask. Good luck, and I'm here to help if you need further guidance!

exchanges.student: Thanks, I'll let you know if I get stuck again.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good! Don't hesitate to reach out if you need more help or have any questions. I'm here to support you in your learning journey. Good luck with your research!

exchanges.student: Thanks, I appreciate it. I'll get back to it now.
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! I'm glad I could help. Best of luck with your assignment, and remember, I'm here if you need any more guidance. You've got this!

exchanges.student: Thanks! I'll keep that in mind.
exchanges.tutor: Great to hear! Keep up the good work, and don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or need further assistance. Take care and good luck with your assignment!
You're welcome! I'm glad I could assist. Remember, I'm here whenever you need help. Have a great day and good luck with your work!
Thank you! Take care and see you next time!

deductions.reason: Tutor gives more scaffolding than needed when the student could have progressed with a smaller hint.
Responses are consistently too long for a tutoring turn (not bite-sized).
Tutor could have more clearly tied a response back to the assignment text or the original question.
